context: Current closing price of AIG (AIG): $77.93 USD as of 2026-04-21.
justification_text:
My point estimates reflect a modest upward drift across time horizons, anchored to what appears to be a current price or index level around 78. At the one-day horizon, the estimate of 78.2 is essentially a near-mean-reversion forecast, assuming no major catalysts are imminent and that intraday momentum carries a very slight positive bias. At the one-week horizon, the slightly higher estimate of 78.5 reflects the assumption that any near-term drift continues at a modest pace, consistent with recent trend behavior. At the one-month horizon, 79.5 incorporates a slightly more optimistic drift assumption while also acknowledging that longer-horizon forecasts are more influenced by fundamental or macro tailwinds that may be gradually accumulating.

The confidence interval widths widen substantially as the horizon extends, which reflects the compounding of uncertainty over time. At one day, the 90% interval spans roughly 5.4 units, which is relatively tight and reflects the relatively predictable short-term behavior of most financial or economic series absent a shock. By one week, the interval widens to 11 units, incorporating the possibility of sentiment shifts, data releases, or positioning changes. At one month, the 23-unit interval reflects the genuine difficulty of forecasting over longer periods, where macro regime changes, policy decisions, and structural shifts all become material contributors to variance.

Several specific risks could push outcomes outside even these relatively wide bounds. On the downside, an unexpected negative macro shock — such as a sharp deterioration in economic data, a geopolitical escalation, or a liquidity event — could drive the value well below 68 over the course of a month. On the upside, a strong positive catalyst such as a surprise policy easing, better-than-expected earnings or data, or a short squeeze dynamic could push well above 91. These tail events are by definition excluded from a 90% interval, meaning there is approximately a 10% probability I assign to outcomes in those extremes collectively.

It is also worth noting that my forecast assumes continuity of the current regime — that is, no structural breaks, no changes in the underlying data-generating process, and no shifts in market microstructure or participant behavior. If any of those assumptions are violated, the intervals should be treated as underestimates of true uncertainty. In practice, interval calibration for financial or index series is notoriously difficult, and historical volatility alone tends to understate realized dispersion during stress periods.
